title: Tables
text:
ImportantKey Rules at a Glance




Source line required on every table — no exceptions
Use — (em dash) for missing values — never leave cells blank
Table titles: headline case · Column headings: sentence case
Full-width layout only — never narrow or centered tables
Maroon header row, alternating light gray body rows

title: Tables
text:
Overview
VVN tables follow strict style guidelines for font styles, font sizes, number formatting, number alignment, and cell shading. These conventions make VVN tables look specific and consistent across all products.
As with VVN charts, every table must have a source line.

For table functions, styling options, and implementation, see the Table Library →

title: Tables
text:
Example


Table Example — Virginia Counties at a Glance (2022)


      




1

Title — Headline case, bold Short and active. Example: “Virginia Counties at a Glance” — not “Table showing Virginia county-level indicators.” Keep to one line where possible.



2

Subtitle — Sentence case (optional) States geography and year. Example: “Virginia, 2022 estimates.” Use to add context not captured in the title.



3

Column header — Maroon background, white text Maroon #861F41 fill, white bold text. Sentence capitalization for standard column headings; headline case for spanner headings (spanning multiple columns); small caps for supra-spanner headings. 1pt black border beneath all column headings and the last row.



4

Alternating rows — Light gray #F7F7F7 Even rows use light gray fill. No internal borders by default. Add row borders (1pt, round dot, #D9D9D9) only when needed for readability. No empty rows or columns.



5

Cell alignment — Decimal-aligned numbers Data cells: decimal-aligned, bottom right. Text cells: bottom left. Format numbers consistently within each column (same decimal places). Never mix alignment within a single column.



6

Source & notes — Required below the table Source line is mandatory. Format: “Source: [Agency, Dataset, Year].” Notes: define abbreviations (“FPL = federal poverty level”), explain methodology, and flag significance. Missing values: em dash (—) — never blank. Significance: *, †, ‡, § in that order. Use superscript letters to link notes to specific cells.

title: Tables
text:
Table Types







Type
Best for




Summary table
Key statistics for a geography or time period


Ranking table
Ordered comparison across localities


Regression table
Statistical model results with significance stars


Crosstabulation
Two-variable frequency or rate breakdowns


Data dictionary
Variable definitions and coding notes


Interactive table
Dashboard exploration with filtering and download




For styled table functions, interactive table setup, and code examples, see the Table Library →

title: Brand
text:
ImportantKey Rules at a Glance




Never use colors outside the VVN palette in charts, maps, tables, or layouts
Use VT Maroon #861F41 for primary headings, navbars, and accents; VT Orange #E5751F for secondary highlights only
Body text is always Charcoal #3D3D3D — never pure black #000000
Chart and map titles: headline case · Subtitles and axis labels: sentence case
Use the accessible palette when colorblind safety is critical

title: Brand
text:
Color Palette

Brand Colors






VT Maroon #861F41 Primary — navbars, titles, chart fill







VT Orange #E5751F Secondary — highlights, buttons, callouts







Navy #1B5299 Tertiary — chart series, accent







Charcoal #3D3D3D Body text, axis labels







Gray #AAAAAA Captions, secondary text







Light Gray #F7F7F7 Backgrounds, sidebar, table rows



The three colors used in charts and multi-series visualizations are Maroon, Orange, and Navy — access them via scale_fill_vvn("brand").



Palettes








Palette
Type
Use




brand
Categorical
3-group comparisons — maroon, orange, navy


main
Categorical
General purpose — up to 10 categories


accessible
Categorical
Colorblind-safe — use when accessibility is critical


maroon_seq
Sequential
Primary continuous metric — choropleth maps


orange_seq
Sequential
Secondary continuous metric


navy_seq
Sequential
Tertiary continuous metric


diverging
Diverging
Data with a meaningful midpoint (above/below average)









WarningColorblind Safety



VT Maroon and VT Orange can appear identical under red-green colorblindness (~8% of men). When both appear on the same chart, differentiate with shape or line type in addition to color, or use scale_fill_vvn("accessible").



For palette functions, see the vvnthemes R Library →

title: Brand
text:
Typography

Fonts and Sizes










Style
Font
Size
Color
Use




Heading
Inter Bold
H1 32px · H2 24px · H3 18px
Maroon #861F41
Titles, section headings, tab labels


Body
Inter Regular
16px
Charcoal #3D3D3D
Paragraphs, axis labels, data labels


Caption
Inter Regular
14px
Gray #AAAAAA
Source lines, notes





Capitalization Rules








Element
Rule
Example




Chart title
Headline case
“Labor Force Participation Rate by Region”


Chart subtitle
Sentence case
“Virginia counties, 2018–2023”


Axis labels
Sentence case
“Percentage of households”


Map title
Headline case
“Median Household Income by County, Virginia”


Map subtitle
Sentence case
“2020 American Community Survey 5-year estimates”


Table title
Headline case
“Summary Statistics by County”


Column headings
Sentence case
“Poverty rate (%)”

title: Brand
text:
Logo Usage

Always use the official VT logo alongside VVN branding
Do not alter, recolor, or reposition the logo
Minimum clear space: equal to the height of the “V” in “Virginia” on all sides
The tagline “Invent the Future®” appears beneath the logo on branded materials

title: Publishing Checklist
text:
ImportantBefore you start



Search your file for [ — if any placeholder text remains, stop and fill it in first. Never publish with unfilled brackets.

title: Publishing Checklist
text:
Dashboard

See Dashboard Library → and Interactive Components →

Complete all items before running rsconnect::deployApp().

Layout & navigation

Navbar background: VT Maroon #861F41 · white project name, bold · orange bottom border 3 px
Exactly 3 KPI cards per row — one card per key metric, colors maroon / orange / navy
Each KPI card has: metric value · short label · optional trend indicator (up / down / flat) with comparison period
Sidebar: 260 px wide · background #F7F7F7
Every filter has a tooltip (ⓘ icon) explaining what it controls and when data was last updated
Apply Filters button: maroon #861F41, full width · Reset button directly below (outline style)
Page footer: white text on maroon · “Visualizing Virginia Numbers · Virginia Tech”



Tab structure

Tab 1 — Overview: sidebar filters + 3 KPI cards + trend line chart + comparison bar chart + distribution chart
Tab 2 — Map: Leaflet choropleth · Virginia county-level · WGS84 (CRS 4326) · maroon_seq or diverging palette · legend inside panel · tooltip shows county name + value + range on hover
Tab 3 — Data: paginated, searchable, sortable DT table · responds to sidebar filters · CSV download button · Excel download button · no raw IDs or processing artifacts
Tab 4 — About: 1–2 paragraph project description + full data source citations + methods note + contact email



All figures inside the dashboard

Every chart: theme_vvn() applied · title (headline case) · subtitle (sentence case) · source line in caption
Only VVN palette colors — no off-palette colors anywhere
Map data normalized — rate or percentage, never raw counts



Publish

No [PLACEHOLDER] text remains
rsconnect::deployApp() runs without errors

title: Publishing Checklist
text:
VVN Insights

See VVN Insight Library →

Complete all items before running quarto render index.qmd.

Document sections (required in order)

1 — Hero banner: #861F41 background · “VVN INSIGHTS” label in small caps · title white bold (headline case) · one-sentence description · publication date + primary data source below headline
2 — Key Finding: 1–2 sentences immediately after the hero · the single most important result · key number bolded — not buried in the conclusion
3 — Background: why this topic matters for Virginia · specific question addressed · intended audience named
4 — Data & Methods: source table (Source / Geography / Period / Variable) + callout noting caveats or comparability issues
5 — Findings: at least 2 charts · 1 map (interactive or static) · 1 summary table · organized into subsections by theme or geographic pattern
6 — Discussion: interpretation (what findings mean, not just what they show) + prior research context + equity implications + limitations
7 — Conclusion: 2–3 sentence takeaway + policy implication or future direction + contact information + VVN and Virginia Tech attribution



Figures in the Insight

Every figure referenced in the narrative before it appears — “Figure 1 shows…”
Every chart: title (headline case) · subtitle (sentence case) · source line
Every map: normalized data · title · legend · source line
Every table: maroon header · alternating rows · em dash — for missing values · source line
KPI callout boxes used only for the most important statistics (orange accent)



Publish

embed-resources: true set in the document YAML
No [PLACEHOLDER] text remains
quarto render index.qmd completes with 0 errors · 0 warnings

title: Publishing Checklist
text:
All Figures
These rules apply to every chart, map, and table in both product types.

Required elements — every figure

Title — headline case (capitalize all major words)
Subtitle — sentence case · states geography and/or year
Source line — “Source: [Agency, Dataset, Year].” — no exceptions
Unit stated once only — subtitle or axis label, never both



Charts

See Chart Library →


theme_vvn() applied to all ggplot2 figures
Only VVN palette colors: Maroon #861F41 · Orange #E5751F · Navy #1B5299
Legend title present if the chart shows multiple series
Notes appear below the source line when abbreviations or methodology need explanation
Significance stars use symbols in order: *, †, ‡, § — never use asterisks for general notes



Maps

See Map Library →


Data normalized — rate or percentage, never raw counts on a choropleth
Sequential data: maroon_seq (primary) or orange_seq (secondary)
Above/below-average data: diverging palette
Categorical / discrete groups: main or accessible palette
Interactive (Leaflet): WGS84 CRS 4326 · tooltip shows county name + value + range
Static (ggplot2 + sf): Albers Equal-Area CRS 5070



Tables

See Table Library →


Maroon #861F41 header row, white bold text
Column headings: sentence case · spanner headings: headline case
Alternating light gray #F7F7F7 body rows · no internal borders by default
Numbers decimal-aligned (bottom right) · text left-aligned (bottom left)
Em dash — for all missing values — never leave a cell blank
Abbreviations defined below the table (e.g., “FPL = federal poverty level”)
Significance noted with superscript letters linked to footnotes

title: Dashboard
text:
ImportantKey Rules at a Glance




Maroon navbar full width across the top; sidebar is 260 px wide and light gray
Three KPI cards per row — one card per key metric, no more
Every filter set must have a corresponding Apply Filters button
All charts and tables inside the dashboard follow the same title/source/capitalization rules as standalone figures

title: Dashboard
text:
Overview
The Interactive Data Visualizations dashboard is an R Shiny application that provides an exploratory interface for VVN data. It uses a maroon navigation bar, sidebar filters, KPI summary cards, charts, an interactive county map, and a downloadable data table.
Use create_vvn_dashboard() from the vvnthemes library to scaffold this structure with one command.

To scaffold and build a dashboard, see the Dashboard Library →

title: Dashboard
text:
Example


Dashboard Example — Broadband Access in Virginia


      




1

Navbar — VT Maroon, full width Background: maroon #861F41. Project name in white bold text. Tab links in white at 90% opacity. Orange bottom border (3px). Navigation tabs clearly labeled.



2

KPI Cards — Three per row Large metric value, short label, and optional trend indicator (up/down/flat). Use maroon, orange, or navy — one color per card, consistently applied across the dashboard.



3

Sidebar Filters — 260px, light gray Background: #F7F7F7. Contains dropdowns, range sliders, and Apply / Reset buttons. Always label each filter clearly and include a tooltip explaining what it controls.



4

Choropleth Map — maroon_seq palette Full-panel interactive Leaflet map on the Map tab. County-level fill. Tooltip shows county name and value. Legend inside panel.



5

Charts — VVN themes throughout All charts follow VVN standards: white background, maroon titles, VVN color palettes, source line in caption. Apply vvnthemes → to all.



6

Footer — Visualizing Virginia Numbers · Virginia Tech Thin footer bar: white text on maroon background. Include VVN name and Virginia Tech affiliation.

title: Dashboard
text:
Tab Structure
The dashboard template has four tabs. Each tab has a defined purpose and required elements.

Tab 1 — Overview
The landing page. Combines sidebar filters, KPI cards, and charts into a single exploratory view.







Element
Details




Sidebar filters
At least one dropdown + one range slider. Apply button (maroon, full width) and Reset button (outline) required.


KPI cards
3 cards (maroon, orange, navy). Each card needs a value, a short label, and an optional directional trend indicator (up / down / flat) with a comparison period.


Trend chart
Line chart of the primary metric over time. Title (headline case), subtitle (sentence case), and source line required.


Comparison chart
Bar or column chart of the latest-year comparison across categories. Title, subtitle, and source line required.


Distribution chart
Histogram or boxplot of the primary metric distribution. Title, subtitle, and source line required.





Tab 2 — Map
Full-panel county-level interactive choropleth map of Virginia.



Element
Details




Choropleth map
Virginia county-level Leaflet map. Projection: WGS84 (CRS 4326). Use maroon_seq for sequential data; diverging for above/below-average data. See Map Library →


Legend
Value breaks clearly labeled; placed inside the map panel.


Tooltip
County name, metric value, and data range on hover. Format values consistently (e.g., “45.2%”, “$129,456”).


Variable selector
Allow users to switch the mapped variable. The map panel header must name the variable displayed.





Tab 3 — Data
Downloadable, searchable table of the underlying data.



Element
Details




Interactive data table
Paginated, searchable, sortable DT table. Responds to sidebar filters. Include only relevant columns — no raw IDs or processing artifacts. See Table Library →


CSV download
Download button for the filtered dataset.


Excel download
Download button for the filtered dataset.


Column formatting
Numbers right-aligned; text left-aligned. Column headings use sentence capitalization. Apply color-bar to the primary numeric column for visual scanning.





Tab 4 — About
Project documentation for transparency and reproducibility.







Element
Details




Project description
1–2 paragraphs explaining the dashboard’s purpose and audience.


Data sources
Bulleted list with full citations for all data used.


Methods
Brief description of any data processing or analytical decisions.


Contact
VVN contact email or link.

title: Dashboard
text:
Filter Panel Requirements


Filter Panel Example — Sidebar Filter Controls


    




1

Filter header — Maroon #861F41 “Filters” label in white bold text. Include a Clear All reset link and funnel icon. Full-width maroon header bar.



2

Dropdown — Clear label + info tooltip Each filter has a bold label above the input and an ⓘ icon that opens a tooltip explaining what the filter does and how data updates annually.



3

Info tooltip — Explain each filter Always include a tooltip on each filter explaining what it controls, what the choices mean, and when the data was last updated.



4

Apply Filters button — Maroon, full width Primary action button. Always pair with a Reset button directly below in outline style. Use Interactive Component Library →




For the full annotated scaffolding, filter components, and KPI card options, see the Dashboard Library → and Interactive Components →

title: Charts
text:
ImportantKey Rules at a Glance




Every chart needs a title (headline case), source line, and axis labels — no exceptions
Subtitles and all axis text use sentence case — never headline case
State the unit of measurement once only — in the subtitle or the axis label, never both
Use only VVN color palettes — never introduce custom colors
Reference every chart in the text before it appears

title: Charts
text:
Overview
Charts are used to visualize data and reveal patterns. All VVN charts use a white background, VT Maroon titles, and VVN color palettes. Every chart must be self-explanatory — a reader should understand the key message without needing to read the surrounding text.

For implementation details, chart functions, and R examples, see the Chart Library →

title: Charts
text:
Example


Chart Example — Virginia Population by Age Group


       




1

Title — Headline case, left-aligned Use active language that draws a conclusion where possible. Maroon #861F41, bold. Keep to one line. State the unit of measurement only in the subtitle or y-axis label — never both. Example: “The Labor Force Participation Rate Has Declined for Men” — not “Labor Force Participation Rate, Men, 1950–2024.”



2

Subtitle — Sentence case, left-aligned Qualifies the title with time period, geography, units, or frequency. Never use headline case. Examples: “Virginia counties, 2018–2023” · “Percent of households with broadband access.”



3

Axis titles and labels — Sentence case First letter and proper nouns capitalized only. Example: “Percentage of total funding,” “2021 operating expenses.” Abbreviations are acceptable if defined in the Notes line below the chart.



4

Legend — Top of chart, between title and y-axis Stretch the legend across the top. Order series logically, mirroring the data order in the chart. When possible, directly label the data and omit the legend entirely.



5

Data labels — Close to the data point Place at the top or inside a bar, or at the end of a line. Match the chart units. Use selectively — to explain important or surprising values, not to repeat obvious ones.



6

Source line — Required on every chart Format: “Source: [Author, Title, Publication Date].” If based on VVN analysis, write “Authors’ analysis.” Set in small gray text at bottom left.



7

Notes — Below source line (when needed) Use for methodology, statistical explanations, and abbreviation definitions. Format: “FPL = federal poverty level.” Use superscript letters (not asterisks) to link notes to specific cells. Reserve asterisks for significance levels only (e.g., * = significant at 0.001 level).

title: Charts
text:
Chart Types
Choose the chart type that best matches your data and analytical goal:








Chart type
Best for
When to avoid




Horizontal bar
Comparing ≥ 5 categories
Continuous data or time series


Vertical column
Comparing 3–4 categories
Long category labels


Line
Trends over time, multiple series
Fewer than 3 data points


Scatter
Relationships between two variables
More than 5 overlapping series


Histogram
Distribution of one variable
Categorical data


Boxplot
Comparing distributions across groups
Audiences unfamiliar with the format


Stacked bar
Part-to-whole across categories
More than 4 categories in a stack




For chart functions, color palettes, and code examples, see the Chart Library → and vvnthemes Package →

title: VVN Style Guide
text:
Visualizing Virginia Numbers · Virginia Tech



The definitive design standard for every product published under the Visualizing Virginia Numbers initiative at Virginia Tech.
This guide defines the design and content standards for every VVN dashboard and Insight post. Start by choosing your product type below, then follow the 6-step workflow. Use the sidebar to find standards for specific components — charts, maps, tables, and brand.

title: VVN Style Guide
text:
What Are You Building?
VVN produces two types of products. Choose yours to begin:


Dashboard
An interactive R Shiny application — maroon navbar, sidebar filters, KPI cards, charts, a county choropleth map, and a searchable data table.
Dashboard Standards →


VVN Insights
A Quarto HTML document that tells a data story — hero banner, narrative, charts, an interactive map, and a summary table. Built for reading, not filtering.
Insights Standards →

title: VVN Style Guide
section: How to Build a VVN Product
text:
How to Build a VVN Product
Follow these six steps in order — every step links to the relevant section of this guide or the R Library.



1


Choose your project typeDashboards are for exploration — interactive filtering, maps, and KPI cards. VVN Insights are for reading — a narrative data story with figures.Dashboard →VVN Insights →







2


Scaffold with the VVN libraryOne R command from the vvnthemes library creates the project with all required files, folders, styles, and placeholder content already in place.Dashboard Library →VVN Insight Library →







3


Load and prepare your dataStore raw files in data/. All cleaning and derived variables go in the data prep script — not in chart code. Document every source.







4


Build figures following VVN standardsEvery chart, map, and table needs a title (headline case), subtitle (sentence case), and source line. Apply theme_vvn() and VVN palettes to all figures — never use off-palette colors.Charts →Maps →Tables →Brand →







5


Write the narrativeLead with the key finding. Reference every figure in the text before it appears (“Figure 1 shows…”). Keep paragraphs to 3–4 sentences. Include background, methods, and limitations.







6


PublishDashboard: deploy via rsconnect::deployApp()  ·  VVN Insight: run quarto render index.qmd with embed-resources: true in the YAML.Dashboard Library →VVN Insight Library →

title: VVN Style Guide
text:
Design Reference
Every figure in both products must follow these standards:


Brand
Colors, typography, and logo usage — the visual foundation every VVN product is built on.
Brand Guidelines →


Charts
Bar, line, scatter, histogram, and boxplot standards with annotated examples.
Chart Standards →


Maps
Choropleth, point, and interactive map standards with annotated examples.
Map Standards →


Tables
Summary and data table standards with annotated examples.
Table Standards →



For R functions, code templates, and implementation details, see the VVN R Library Documentation →

title: Maps
text:
ImportantKey Rules at a Glance




Always normalize counts before mapping — use rates or percentages, never raw counts
Every map needs a title (headline case), subtitle, legend, and source line
Reference every map in the text before it appears
Use interactive maps (leaflet) for dashboards; static maps (ggplot2 + sf) for Insights and reports

title: Maps
text:
Overview
Maps visualize geographic patterns and help users explore spatial data. All VVN maps use consistent colors, clear legends, and informative popups. The map should be referenced in the text before it appears.
Choose the map type based on your data structure and whether interactivity is required (dashboards vs. Insights).

For map functions, color scales, and interactive map implementation, see the Map Library →

title: Maps
text:
Example


Map Example — Median Household Income by County, Virginia


       




1

Title — Headline case, left-aligned Clearly names the variable and geography. Example: “Median Household Income by County, Virginia.” Define any abbreviations used.



2

Subtitle — Sentence case States the data source and vintage year. Example: “2020 American Community Survey (ACS) 5-Year Estimates.”



3

Legend — Inside map panel, clearly labeled Use maroon_seq for sequential (continuous) data. Use diverging for above/below-average data. Include clear break labels. The label must include the variable name and units (e.g., “Median income ($)”).



4

Tooltip — Interactive maps only Show the county name, the metric value, and the data range on hover. Format values consistently (e.g., “$129,456” or “45.2%”). Static maps do not need a tooltip.



5

Source line — Required on every map Format: “Source: [Agency], [Dataset], [Year].” Add a Notes line to define abbreviations, explain missing counties, or flag data suppression.



6

Basemap — Muted, light gray Use a neutral basemap so the data layer is the visual focus. Projection: WGS84 (CRS 4326) for interactive Leaflet maps; Albers Equal-Area (CRS 5070) for static ggplot maps of Virginia.



7

Color palette — Match to data type Sequential (rates, percentages): maroon_seq (primary), orange_seq (secondary). Diverging (above/below midpoint): diverging. Categorical (discrete groups): main or accessible. Never use off-palette colors.

title: Maps
text:
Map Types
Choose the map type that best represents your data:








Type
Interactive?
Best for




Choropleth
Optional
Rates, percentages, or index values by county or region


Point map
Optional
Specific locations — facilities, events, sample sites


Bubble map
No
Proportional quantities at geographic locations


Dot-density
No
Population or entity distribution across space


Tile-grid map
No
Equal-weight comparisons across localities


Interactive (Leaflet)
Yes
Dashboards and web-based exploration




For static map functions, interactive Leaflet setup, and code examples, see the Map Library →

title: VVN Insights
text:
ImportantKey Rules at a Glance




Lead with the finding — state the most important result in the first paragraph
Reference every figure in the text before it appears: “Figure 1 shows…”
Search for [ before publishing — no placeholder text in the final output
Insights are for reading, not exploration — use the Dashboard for interactive filtering

title: VVN Insights
text:
Overview
A VVN Insight is a Quarto HTML document that tells a data story. It combines a narrative with charts, an interactive map, and a summary table — all styled to the VVN design standard. Insights are designed for reading, not exploration.
Use create_vvn_story() from the vvnthemes library to scaffold this structure with one command.

To scaffold and build a VVN Insight, see the VVN Insight Library →

title: VVN Insights
text:
Example


VVN Insights Example — Broadband Access Across Virginia’s Counties


       




1

Hero banner — Full-width maroon Background: VT Maroon #861F41. Contains “VVN INSIGHTS” label in small caps, the full insight title in white bold (headline case), and a one-sentence description of the topic and geography. Subtitle reads “Visualizing Virginia Numbers · Virginia Tech” at 70% white opacity.



2

Headline — White, headline case, bold States the topic and scope. Example: “Broadband Access Across Virginia’s Counties.” Should communicate the subject immediately.



3

Publication date and source tag Publication date and primary data source appear below the headline. Include the dataset name and vintage year.



4

Choropleth map — County level Primary geographic visualization. Uses maroon_seq palette. Interactive with tooltip showing county name and value. Referenced in the surrounding text.



5

KPI callout — Orange accent Key statistics highlighted in orange callout boxes. Use for the most important numbers only. Example: “76% of Virginia households have broadband.”



6

Narrative — Left-aligned, structured sections Body text in Charcoal #3D3D3D. Short paragraphs, 3–4 sentences. Clear section headings in sentence case. Every figure referenced in the text before it appears.



7

Source line — Required on every figure Every chart, map, and table must have a source line below it. Format: “Source: [Agency, Dataset, Year]. [Percentage note or additional detail].”

title: VVN Insights
text:
Document Structure
Every VVN Insight must follow this section order:









#
Section
Required
Content Requirements




1
Hero Banner
Yes
“VVN INSIGHTS” label · headline (headline case) · one-sentence description of topic and geography · “Visualizing Virginia Numbers · Virginia Tech” in subtitle


2
Key Finding
Yes
1–2 sentences · state the metric, geography, time period, and direction of change · use blockquote (>) or :::{.callout-important} · bold the key number


3
Background
Yes
Why this topic matters for Virginia · policy relevance · the specific question this Insight addresses · intended audience (policymakers, advocates, community organizations)


4
Data & Methods
Yes
Data source table (Source, Geography, Period, Variable) · callout noting caveats or comparability issues


5
Findings
Yes
Subsections by theme · each subsection: heading (sentence case) + 1–3 paragraphs + at least one figure · minimum: 2 charts, 1 map, 1 summary table · every figure referenced in text before it appears


6
Discussion
Yes
Interpretation of findings · connection to prior research or policy context · equity implications · limitations (data gaps, geography, causal claims)


7
Conclusion
Yes
2–3 sentence takeaway · policy implication or future work direction · VVN contact info and Virginia Tech attribution



See Chart Standards → · Map Standards → · Table Standards →

For the full annotated scaffolding with all sections pre-built, see the VVN Insight Library →
